common_name: Foxtail Orchid (Gigantea Rhynchostylis, Giant Vanda, Foxtail Vanda)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork or tree fern bark, or in a slatted teak basket with minimal or no media (this species grows best with roots fully exposed to air); Rhynchostylis gigantea is a monopodial orchid that should never be repotted — simply mount or attach to new material as the plant outgrows its mounting. In zone 9, grow on a shaded porch in a hanging basket year-round, protecting from temperatures below 50°F. Natural blooming occurs in fall–winter in the Northern Hemisphere. Water by misting or dunking the roots daily in summer, less frequently in winter. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle direct morning sun with fully exposed aerial roots — Rhynchostylis gigantea, like its Vanda relatives, requires bright light and excellent air circulation to produce its best flower display. Rhynchostylis gigantea is a large, monopodial epiphytic orchid from the tropical forests of Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Borneo in the family Orchidaceae, closely related to Vanda and considered by many orchid enthusiasts to produce the most intensely fragrant flowers of any readily available epiphytic orchid. The plant produces a single upright stem clothed in pairs of thick, strap-like, semi-terete dark-green leaves, with long, pendulous, rope-like silver roots hanging below. In fall and winter, the plants produce pendant racemes (the 'foxtails') 8–16 inches long, densely packed with 30–80 small (1–1.5 inch) waxy flowers in white to pale pink with distinctive spots and bars of deep pink-magenta to red-purple — the entire drooping, densely-flowered raceme resembling a fox's bushy tail. The fragrance is extraordinary — sweet, powerful, and pervasive, carrying remarkable distances on warm evenings, often described as combining jasmine, honey, and citrus. Rhynchostylis gigantea is the national flower of Myanmar (Burma). White-flowering albino forms ('Alba') are particularly prized. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Orchid Society — Rhynchostylis gigantea cultivation guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Rhynchostylis cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vanda alliance orchids for South Florida
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Rhynchostylis (non-toxic)

common_name: Bower Vine (Bower of Beauty, Pink Bower Vine, Jasmine Pandorea)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring on a trellis, pergola, fence, or arbor in full sun to partial shade; Bower Vine grows rapidly in zone 9 and begins blooming in spring. It is more cold-tolerant than many tropical vines, surviving temperatures to 28°F briefly — established plants in zone 9a often survive winters and resprout from the crown. Prune lightly after the main spring bloom to shape; avoid hard pruning in fall which removes next year's flower buds. The cultivar 'Lady Di' (pure white flowers) and 'Rosea' (deep pink) are widely available. Propagate from stem cuttings.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Bower Vine blooms most prolifically in full sun but performs well in partial shade and actually benefits from afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat. Pandorea jasminoides is a vigorous, twining, evergreen vine native to eastern Australia (Queensland and New South Wales) in the Bignoniaceae family, one of the finest Australian native vines for subtropical and warm-temperate gardens worldwide. The plant produces attractive, pinnately-compound leaves with 5–9 leaflets that create a fine-textured, airy, dark-green canopy on whatever structure the vine covers. In spring and intermittently through the warm months, the vine produces clusters of funnel-shaped to trumpet-shaped flowers in shades of white to soft pink, typically with a distinct deep rose-pink to rose-red interior throat — the clean, elegant color combination has made Bower Vine one of the most popular ornamental vines for zone 9-11 gardens, walls, and arbors. The flowers are lightly fragrant in warm conditions. Multiple cultivars are available: 'Rosea' (deeper pink), 'Lady Di' (pure white with no pink throat), and 'Rosea Superba' (larger, deeper flowers). Bower Vine is relatively free of pest and disease problems in zone 9 conditions, making it one of the most reliable and beautiful low-maintenance ornamental vines for Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pandorea jasminoides for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pandorea jasminoides for Gulf Coast zone 9
Royal Horticultural Society — Pandorea jasminoides cultivation
LSU AgCenter — Ornamental Vines for Gulf Coast Louisiana

common_name: White Cedar (Pink Trumpet Tree, Variable-Leaf Tabebuia, Caribbean Lapacho)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Handroanthus heterophyllus is one of the more cold-tolerant Handroanthus species, surviving temperatures to 24–28°F with some damage. In zone 9, established trees produce reliable spring bloom and may recover well from cold damage. Like other trumpet trees, it blooms best on bare branches after a dry period. Semi-deciduous — the leaves drop before spring bloom in cool or dry conditions. The common name 'White Cedar' reflects historical Caribbean timber use; the wood is extremely hard and durable.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile soil — White Cedar is drought-tolerant once established and performs well in the sandy, alkaline soils of coastal Gulf and Florida landscapes. Handroanthus heterophyllus (formerly Tabebuia heterophylla) is a medium-sized, semi-deciduous flowering tree native to the Caribbean islands (Puerto Rico, Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica, and the Lesser Antilles) in the family Bignoniaceae, the national tree of Puerto Rico. The species name 'heterophyllus' (variable leaf) refers to the variation in leaf form on individual trees — sometimes simple, sometimes 3-leaflet, sometimes 5-leaflet compound. In spring, the tree produces clusters of large (3–4 inch), trumpet-shaped flowers in soft lavender-pink to light rose-pink, blooming on bare or nearly bare branches before the new leaf flush — the delicate pink coloration is softer and more pastel than the vibrant magenta-pink of H. impetiginosus, creating a distinctly elegant bloom display. The tree is widely used in Caribbean and tropical American landscapes as a medium-sized flowering shade tree, and has proven successful in zone 9 Gulf South conditions where its relative cold tolerance, drought adaptability, and spring flower display make it one of the better choices among the many beautiful but sometimes tender Handroanthus trumpet trees.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Handroanthus heterophyllus (White Cedar)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tabebuia species for Gulf Coast zone 9
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Handroanthus heterophyllus
LSU AgCenter — Flowering Trees for Gulf Coast Louisiana Landscapes

common_name: Argentine Trumpet Vine (Garrocha Tecoma, Red Esperanza)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Tecoma garrocha is cold-hardy to zone 7-8 (20–25°F) — more cold-tolerant than T. stans. It grows as a large sprawling shrub or small tree in zone 9. The long, tubular flowers are outstanding hummingbird attractants. Prune by one-third in late winter to shape and stimulate new flowering stems. Can be trained as a small tree with staking. The orange-red flower color is somewhat different from yellow-flowered T. stans, offering a warmer-toned alternative. Propagate from stem cuttings or seed.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Tecoma garrocha grows most vigorously in full sun and will produce fewer flowers and become leggy in shade. Tecoma garrocha is a large, fast-growing, semi-deciduous shrub or small tree native to Argentina and Bolivia in the Bignoniaceae family, closely related to the commonly grown Yellow Bells (T. stans) but with distinctly orange-red to coral-red tubular flowers rather than yellow — a color difference with significant ornamental consequence. The plant produces compound leaves of several lance-shaped leaflets and bears its trumpet-shaped flowers in terminal clusters throughout the warm months from spring through fall, with a particularly prolific bloom in summer and early fall. The long, tubular, orange-red flowers are extraordinary hummingbird attractants — the nectar-rich, hummingbird-adapted flower form and vivid color ensures a continuous parade of hummingbirds in zone 9 gardens where this shrub is planted. Unlike many tropical-looking flowering shrubs, Tecoma garrocha is genuinely cold-hardy — cold-hardy to zone 7-8, surviving temperatures to 20°F — one of the most cold-hardy of the Tecoma species. This cold tolerance combined with its spectacular hummingbird value and long bloom season makes T. garrocha one of the most underused and undervalued ornamental shrubs for zone 8-9 Gulf Coast landscapes.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Tecoma garrocha (Argentine Trumpet Vine)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tecoma garrocha for Gulf Coast hummingbird gardens
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Tropical Shrubs for Louisiana Landscapes
University of Georgia Extension — Tecoma species for Southeast gardens

common_name: Yellow Mangosteen (Mysore Gamboge, Yellow Gamboge, Egg Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in partial shade with consistently moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil; Garcinia xanthochymus is frost-sensitive but somewhat more cold-tolerant than the Purple Mangosteen (G. mangostana), surviving brief temperatures to 32°F if the soil drains well. In zone 9b-10, it can be tried as a large container or borderline landscape tree in sheltered spots. The yellow fruits ripen summer–fall; the sweet-sour flesh is eaten fresh or used in preserves and beverages. Trees are self-fertile — a single tree produces fruit.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil; Yellow Mangosteen grows best in the filtered light and moist, rich conditions of its native South Asian forest habitat and benefits from shade protection during establishment. Garcinia xanthochymus is an attractive, medium-sized, evergreen tree native to the Western Ghats and Mysore region of India and adjacent Burma (Myanmar) in the family Clusiaceae — the same family as the famous Purple Mangosteen (G. mangostana). The tree has a naturally elegant form: a pyramidal to columnar crown of large, glossy, deep-green, lance-shaped leaves that hang in graceful, slightly pendant arrays from the branches. In summer, the tree produces smooth, round to slightly oval fruits 2–4 inches in diameter with bright golden-yellow skin when ripe, containing 1–4 large seeds surrounded by soft, translucent, sweet-sour, juicy pulp. The flavor is distinctive — a complex sweet-sour combination reminiscent of both apricot and tamarind, refreshing as a fresh fruit and excellent in juices, jams, and chutneys. Yellow Mangosteen is considered by tropical fruit enthusiasts to be more adaptable to cultivation outside the tropics than the famously difficult Purple Mangosteen — it tolerates a somewhat wider range of soil conditions and performs in zone 10 where G. mangostana struggles. Also grown as an ornamental specimen tree for its attractive form and handsome foliage.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Garcinia species for South Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Garcinia xanthochymus cultivation notes
Hawaii Tropical Fruit Growers — Garcinia xanthochymus: Yellow Mangosteen
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Garcinia xanthochymus horticultural notes
